source_title: Exor: Fiat Crisis to Ferrari Glory - [Business Breakdowns, EP.229]

paragraphs.speaker: zack
paragraphs.name: Zack Fuss
paragraphs.text: Today we are breaking down Exor. The origins of Exor date back to the end of the 19th century when Giovanni Agnelli founded the auto company Fiat. Over the company's 100-year-old history, a lot has changed. While some of the core assets remain the same, today Exor serves as an investment holding company. Its largest and most notable assets are Ferrari, CNH, Stellantis, Philips, and a number of other notable private companies.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: Exor is a Dutch holding company controlled by the Agnelli family. Giovanni Agnelli was the founder of FIAT in the late 18th century, and their family wealth is managed through this holding company. It's intimately tied with the history of industry in Italy. Throughout the 20th century FIAT was the industrial engineer of the country. 80% of the Italian auto market was FIAT. The FIAT 500 was iconic, launched in 1957, huge global commercial success, and put Italian engineering and design on the map.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: You can look at the history of this company through three leaders who all have had an outsized impact. Two of them were called Giovanni Agnelli, and the third is John Elkann, who leads the group today. The founder transformed FIAT from just a startup carmaker to a large industrial conglomerate. Then you have the second Giovanni, the grandson, called Gianni. He transformed FIAT into a global company over a period of almost 40 years at the helm. Crucially, he formed the partnership with Ferrari, which over the years has become a masterstroke for the family's fortunes.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: John Elkann is the grandson of Gianni and great, great grandson of Giovanni, the founder. At just 21, he was nominated to inherit the leadership from his grandfather. Exor today was set up in 2009 to simplify how the family's assets were managed. Before that, it was a complex mess. The original holding company goes all the way back to 1927. With Exor, everything came under a single country regulatory authority. The legal headquarters was moved to the Netherlands. You get full exemption on capital gains and dividends, a very competitive tax rate, extensive tax treaties globally.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: Today, if you look at Exor, you've got stakes in listed global companies, and then you have the private investments. It's weighted very much towards the publics, 70% of gross asset value. Gross asset value is roughly 45 billion euros. 70% of that is in four companies: Ferrari, Stellantis, CNHI, which is Case New Holland, and Philips. The rest is in private investments, so a range of investments there: Louboutin and Shang Xia in luxury, The Economist magazine, Welltec, and an asset management firm called Lingoto.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: When Elkann comes into the thick of action at Fiat, things were really, really bad. At the ripe age of 27, he becomes the sole family representative of the firm. And at that time, both financially and operationally, the company was struggling. They've been through four CEOs in three years, and for a while, it looked like the firm won't survive. One of the first things he does was play a role in the selection of Sergio Marchionne as CEO of Fiat. This was very controversial at the time because Marchionne was an outsider, but it ended up being a brilliant decision. Marchionne really went to work at Fiat.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: Ferrari is the largest asset. The ethos of Ferrari is motor racing, and selling cars is almost an afterthought. That's what makes them unique. The market agrees. It's clearly saying this is not a car company. Ferrari started at 15% of NAV and went as high as almost half the portfolio. In February, Exor sold about 3 billion worth of shares with the intent of using the proceeds towards both buybacks and additional investments. They are signaling that Ferrari had grown much beyond their comfortable concentration level of 25% to 30%. They find their stock with an NAV discount to be hugely undervalued.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: The asset base is dominated by publicly listed companies. A full 70% of assets is listed. You can make reasonable assumptions on the private assets, subtract net debt, and you end up with a reasonable estimate of spot NAV. The stock trades at a big discount to this NAV, anywhere from 50% to 60%, depending on your assumptions. Having a NAV discount is part and parcel of being a holding company. In the case of Exor, that discount has been unusually high for a very long period of time.

paragraphs.speaker: krishna
paragraphs.name: Krishna Mohanraj
paragraphs.text: The way they define the investment approach itself is very systematic and very clear. They frame their approach as a duality: entrepreneurial spirit on one side and financial discipline on the other.
